sp-sec9-u1-L1-Q1.1: This is true because Newton's first law guarantees that without external forces like friction, objects in motion continue moving indefinitely.

sp-sec9-u1-L1-Q2.0: Gravity is a force that pulls objects together, not the property that resists changes in motion.
sp-sec9-u1-L1-Q2.2: Friction is a force that opposes motion, not the tendency of objects to resist changes in their state.
sp-sec9-u1-L1-Q2.3: Momentum is the product of mass and velocity, not the resistance to changes in motion.

sp-sec9-u1-L1-Q3.0: In the vacuum of space there is no friction to slow a spacecraft, so engines are not needed to maintain speed.

sp-sec9-u1-L1-Q5.0: Gravity very much exists in orbit; the ISS experiences about 90% of surface gravity but is in free fall.
sp-sec9-u1-L1-Q5.1: The ISS has no special mechanism to create weightlessness; the effect comes from continuous free fall.
sp-sec9-u1-L1-Q5.3: The ISS does not spin to cancel gravity; it orbits Earth in free fall along with everything inside it.

sp-sec9-u1-L2-Q1.1: This is true because F = ma directly shows that doubling force with constant mass doubles acceleration.

sp-sec9-u1-L2-Q2.0: Constant thrust with decreasing mass means acceleration increases, not stays the same.
sp-sec9-u1-L2-Q2.1: Acceleration increases as mass decreases because F = ma means less mass yields more acceleration for the same force.
sp-sec9-u1-L2-Q2.3: The rocket continues moving as long as thrust is applied; losing fuel mass does not stop it.

sp-sec9-u1-L2-Q5.0: Heavier objects accelerate less with the same force, so Rocket A would be slower, not faster.
sp-sec9-u1-L2-Q5.1: With the same force and different masses, the lighter rocket accelerates more, so they are not equal.
sp-sec9-u1-L2-Q5.3: Both rockets can move without gravity; thrust creates acceleration regardless of gravitational environment.

sp-sec9-u1-L2-Q6.1: Mass stays the same regardless of gravitational environment; only weight changes between Earth and the Moon.

sp-sec9-u1-L2-Q7.1: Adding more fuel tanks increases mass, which would decrease acceleration rather than increase it.
sp-sec9-u1-L2-Q7.2: A gravity assist requires precise orbital mechanics and cannot simply be aimed at the Sun from the launch pad.
sp-sec9-u1-L2-Q7.3: Atmospheric pressure has negligible impact on rocket acceleration compared to reducing onboard mass.

sp-sec9-u1-L3-Q1.1: This is true because Newton's third law states the exhaust pushes backward and the rocket is pushed forward with equal force.

sp-sec9-u1-L3-Q2.0: Air in the room plays no role; the balloon moves because escaping air creates a reaction force in the opposite direction.
sp-sec9-u1-L3-Q2.2: Gravity pulls the balloon down uniformly; the erratic motion is caused by the escaping air jet, not gravity.
sp-sec9-u1-L3-Q2.3: Static electricity does not cause balloons to fly around; the thrust comes from air escaping the opening.

sp-sec9-u1-L3-Q3.0: Rockets push against their own exhaust gas, not surrounding air, so they work perfectly in a vacuum.

sp-sec9-u1-L3-Q6.0: Action and reaction forces are always equal, so 500 N would violate Newton's third law.
sp-sec9-u1-L3-Q6.2: The reaction force is exactly equal to the action force, not double; 2,000 N would violate Newton's third law.
sp-sec9-u1-L3-Q6.3: The thrust force is always equal to the exhaust force regardless of the rocket's mass; mass only affects acceleration.

sp-sec9-u2-L1-Q1.1: This is true because thrust is defined as the forward force created by expelling exhaust in the opposite direction.

sp-sec9-u2-L1-Q2.0: Air resistance opposes motion but is not what thrust must overcome for liftoff; gravity is the primary force.
sp-sec9-u2-L1-Q2.1: The launch pad does not exert a force the rocket must overcome; it is Earth's gravitational pull on the rocket.
sp-sec9-u2-L1-Q2.3: Earth's rotation actually helps rockets launched eastward and is not a force that must be overcome for liftoff.

sp-sec9-u2-L1-Q3.0: A ratio of 0.8 means gravity is stronger than thrust, so the rocket cannot lift off the ground.

sp-sec9-u2-L1-Q5.0: 35 / 28 = 1.25, not 0.8; a ratio below 1 would mean the rocket could not lift off.
sp-sec9-u2-L1-Q5.1: 35 / 28 = 1.25, not 1.0; a ratio of exactly 1 would mean thrust just equals weight with no upward acceleration.

sp-sec9-u2-L1-Q6.0: Paint color has no meaningful effect on rocket thrust or engine performance.
sp-sec9-u2-L1-Q6.1: Launching from higher altitude slightly reduces air resistance but does not directly increase thrust.
sp-sec9-u2-L1-Q6.3: Fewer engines would decrease total thrust, not increase it.

sp-sec9-u2-L2-Q1.1: This is true because the payload is the cargo delivered to space, sitting at the top of the rocket.

sp-sec9-u2-L2-Q3.0: 25% fuel would be far too little propellant to reach orbit given the enormous energy requirements.
sp-sec9-u2-L2-Q3.1: 50% fuel is still insufficient; the tyranny of the rocket equation demands much more propellant.
sp-sec9-u2-L2-Q3.2: 70% is closer but still underestimates the typical 85-90% fuel fraction needed to reach orbit.

sp-sec9-u2-L2-Q6.0: Noise reduction is not the purpose of the nozzle shape; it is designed to maximize exhaust velocity.
sp-sec9-u2-L2-Q6.2: While nozzles do need cooling, the bell shape is specifically for accelerating exhaust gas, not for cooling.
sp-sec9-u2-L2-Q6.3: Nozzles do not filter exhaust; their converging-diverging shape accelerates gas to supersonic speeds for maximum thrust.

sp-sec9-u2-L3-Q1.1: This is true because discarding empty fuel tanks reduces dead weight, allowing remaining fuel to be used more efficiently.

sp-sec9-u2-L3-Q2.0: Carrying empty tanks wastes fuel on dead weight, which is the main inefficiency of single-stage designs.
sp-sec9-u2-L3-Q2.1: Single-stage rockets can use multiple fuel types; the limitation is carrying empty structure to orbit.
sp-sec9-u2-L3-Q2.2: Engine size is not the issue; the problem is carrying empty fuel tanks all the way to orbit.
sp-sec9-u2-L3-Q2.3: Single-stage rockets can fly above the atmosphere, but they are less efficient due to carrying dead weight.

sp-sec9-u2-L3-Q5.0: Saturn V was far too complex for a single stage; one stage could not carry enough fuel for a Moon mission.
sp-sec9-u2-L3-Q5.1: Two stages would not have provided enough delta-v for the complex Earth-orbit-to-Moon trajectory.
sp-sec9-u2-L3-Q5.3: Five stages would add unnecessary complexity and weight; three was the optimal design choice.

sp-sec9-u2-L3-Q7.1: Even though the satellite is light, a high orbit requires significant delta-v that benefits from staging efficiency.
sp-sec9-u2-L3-Q7.2: Five stages would add excessive complexity with diminishing returns; two stages is more practical.
sp-sec9-u2-L3-Q7.3: Simply adding fuel without staging means carrying heavy empty tanks, which wastes propellant.

sp-sec9-u3-L1-Q1.1: This is true because gravity continuously pulls the satellite toward Earth while its sideways speed keeps it from hitting the surface.

sp-sec9-u3-L1-Q2.0: Gravity reaches far into space; at ISS altitude (400 km), gravity is still about 90% of surface strength.
sp-sec9-u3-L1-Q2.1: The ISS only fires engines occasionally for small adjustments; it maintains orbit through its sideways velocity.
sp-sec9-u3-L1-Q2.3: There is virtually no atmosphere at ISS altitude to provide any support.

sp-sec9-u3-L1-Q4.0: Engine thrust is not needed to maintain an orbit; satellites coast without engines once in orbit.
sp-sec9-u3-L1-Q4.2: Magnetic fields and solar wind have negligible effects on satellite orbits compared to gravity.
sp-sec9-u3-L1-Q4.3: Fuel and computers help reach orbit but do not maintain it; gravity and velocity do.

sp-sec9-u3-L1-Q5.0: Higher orbits have weaker gravity and longer paths, so satellites there move slower, not faster.

sp-sec9-u3-L2-Q1.1: This is true because LEO is defined as the orbital region from 200 to 2,000 km altitude above Earth.

sp-sec9-u3-L2-Q3.0: All orbiting satellites are technically in free fall and require no fuel to stay in orbit.
sp-sec9-u3-L2-Q3.2: Geostationary satellites are actually the slowest orbital satellites; LEO satellites move much faster.
sp-sec9-u3-L2-Q3.3: GEO at 35,786 km is far from Earth, not close; LEO satellites are the closest.

sp-sec9-u3-L2-Q6.0: Being closer to the Sun has no relevance to Earth observation; orbit type matters for surface coverage.
sp-sec9-u3-L2-Q6.2: Polar orbits are not inherently faster; their advantage is covering all latitudes as Earth rotates beneath.
sp-sec9-u3-L2-Q6.3: No orbit can see in the dark without special instruments; polar orbits are useful for their complete surface coverage.

sp-sec9-u3-L3-Q1.0: Kepler proved that orbits are ellipses, not perfect circles, even if some are nearly circular.

sp-sec9-u3-L3-Q3.0: Planets move slowest at their farthest point from the Sun, not fastest, due to conservation of angular momentum.
sp-sec9-u3-L3-Q3.2: Orbital speed varies continuously; it is never constant throughout an elliptical orbit.
sp-sec9-u3-L3-Q3.3: There is no special speed at the midpoint; speed increases smoothly as the planet approaches the Sun.

sp-sec9-u3-L3-Q4.1: This is true because Earth's closest approach to the Sun occurs in early January, and Kepler's law says planets move faster when closer.

sp-sec9-u3-L3-Q5.0: Twice the distance does not mean twice the period; Kepler's third law involves a cube-square relationship.
sp-sec9-u3-L3-Q5.1: Four times as long would apply if the period scaled with the square of distance, but it scales with the 1.5 power.
sp-sec9-u3-L3-Q5.3: Half as long would mean closer planets orbit slower, which is the opposite of what Kepler's law states.

sp-sec9-u4-L1-Q1.0: The first law explains coasting in space, not the mechanism of thrust generation.
sp-sec9-u4-L1-Q1.1: The second law relates force, mass, and acceleration but does not explain how thrust is created.
sp-sec9-u4-L1-Q1.3: The law of gravitation describes attraction between masses, not the action-reaction principle behind thrust.

sp-sec9-u4-L1-Q2.0: Rockets push against their own exhaust, not surrounding air, so they generate thrust perfectly in vacuum.

sp-sec9-u4-L1-Q5.0: With decreasing mass and constant force, F = ma shows acceleration must increase, not stay the same.
sp-sec9-u4-L1-Q5.1: Acceleration increases as fuel burns because less mass is being pushed by the same thrust.
sp-sec9-u4-L1-Q5.3: Acceleration direction remains the same as thrust direction; it does not reverse.

sp-sec9-u4-L1-Q7.1: A ratio of 0.95 means gravity exceeds thrust, so launching would fail regardless of how close it seems.
sp-sec9-u4-L1-Q7.2: Adding fuel increases total weight along with potential thrust, and the ratio may not improve sufficiently.
sp-sec9-u4-L1-Q7.3: Wind provides negligible force compared to rocket thrust and cannot compensate for insufficient thrust-to-weight ratio.

sp-sec9-u4-L2-Q1.0: While staging can improve aerodynamics slightly, its primary purpose is shedding empty mass.
sp-sec9-u4-L2-Q1.2: Payload protection is handled by the fairing, not by staging.
sp-sec9-u4-L2-Q1.3: Staging does not create more engine power; it removes dead weight so existing power is used more efficiently.

sp-sec9-u4-L2-Q2.1: This is true because GEO satellites orbit at exactly 35,786 km where the orbital period matches Earth's 24-hour rotation.

sp-sec9-u4-L2-Q5.0: The midpoint has no special significance for orbital speed; speed varies continuously based on distance from the Sun.
sp-sec9-u4-L2-Q5.1: Planets move slowest at their farthest point from the Sun, the opposite of what this option states.
sp-sec9-u4-L2-Q5.2: Circular orbits have constant speed, but Kepler's second law applies to elliptical orbits where speed varies with distance.

sp-sec9-u4-L2-Q7.0: Without staging, the extra dead weight would slow the rocket; it would not reach the Moon faster.
sp-sec9-u4-L2-Q7.1: Carrying empty tanks is the opposite of efficient; staging eliminates that dead weight.
sp-sec9-u4-L2-Q7.3: The massive weight of empty tanks would prevent the rocket from reaching orbital velocity.

sp-sec9-u5-L1-Q1.1: This is true because at approximately 28,000 km/h, a spacecraft achieves the balance between gravity and sideways velocity needed for LEO.

sp-sec9-u5-L1-Q2.1: 8 km/s (28,000 km/h) is orbital velocity for LEO, not escape velocity; you would remain in orbit, not escape.
sp-sec9-u5-L1-Q2.2: 3 km/s is far too slow to escape Earth's gravity; it would not even achieve a stable orbit.
sp-sec9-u5-L1-Q2.3: 30 km/s greatly exceeds Earth's escape velocity; that speed would send you far beyond Earth rapidly.

sp-sec9-u5-L1-Q3.0: Orbital velocity is about 7.8 km/s, which is less than the 11.2 km/s escape velocity, so the craft is still gravitationally bound.

sp-sec9-u5-L1-Q5.0: Orbital velocity keeps you circling Earth; to travel to Mars, you must break free of Earth's gravity entirely.
sp-sec9-u5-L1-Q5.2: Half of orbital velocity is far too slow even to maintain orbit, let alone reach another planet.
sp-sec9-u5-L1-Q5.3: The speed of light is neither necessary nor achievable with current technology for interplanetary travel.

sp-sec9-u5-L2-Q1.1: This is true because delta-v represents the total capacity for speed changes, which determines what destinations and maneuvers are possible.

sp-sec9-u5-L2-Q2.0: While producing delta-v does cost money, the analogy refers to budgeting velocity changes like spending currency.
sp-sec9-u5-L2-Q2.2: Delta-v is measured in meters per second, not dollars; it is a physics quantity, not a monetary one.
sp-sec9-u5-L2-Q2.3: Space agencies do not trade delta-v between missions; each mission has its own independent fuel budget.

sp-sec9-u5-L2-Q4.0: The size of the launch pad has no effect on how much velocity change a rocket can achieve.
sp-sec9-u5-L2-Q4.2: Paint color has zero impact on rocket performance or available delta-v.
sp-sec9-u5-L2-Q4.3: Rocket spin rate does not determine total velocity change; only fuel and exhaust velocity matter.

sp-sec9-u5-L2-Q6.0: Due to the rocket equation, each extra kilogram of payload requires many more kilograms of fuel because fuel must also lift itself.

sp-sec9-u5-L2-Q7.1: Communication is important, but adding 50 kg worsens the already tight delta-v budget by increasing total mass.
sp-sec9-u5-L2-Q7.2: Adding more fuel also adds mass, which partly cancels the benefit due to the tyranny of the rocket equation.
sp-sec9-u5-L2-Q7.3: Delta-v depends directly on mass ratio, so reducing mass absolutely does increase available delta-v.

sp-sec9-u5-L3-Q1.0: Rockets must tilt sideways during ascent to build the horizontal speed required for orbit.

sp-sec9-u5-L3-Q2.0: The Sun's radiation has no bearing on rocket trajectory; the tilt is to build horizontal orbital velocity.
sp-sec9-u5-L3-Q2.2: Payload stress reduction is managed by throttling, not by the gravity turn maneuver.
sp-sec9-u5-L3-Q2.3: The tilt is a carefully planned maneuver called a gravity turn, not an uncontrolled fall.

sp-sec9-u5-L3-Q5.0: Fuel savings are secondary; the primary reason for throttling at Max Q is to protect the rocket from structural failure.
sp-sec9-u5-L3-Q5.2: Passenger comfort is not the driving concern; structural integrity of the vehicle is the critical factor.
sp-sec9-u5-L3-Q5.3: Stage separation occurs much later in flight, not during the Max Q throttle-down period.

sp-sec9-u5-L3-Q6.1: This is true because without circularization, the orbit would be elliptical and could dip back into the atmosphere.

sp-sec9-u5-L3-Q7.0: Without enough horizontal speed, a spacecraft cannot maintain any orbit, slow or otherwise; it will fall back.
sp-sec9-u5-L3-Q7.2: Insufficient speed means the craft is still gravitationally bound and will fall toward Earth, not escape.
sp-sec9-u5-L3-Q7.3: Hovering in place is not possible in orbit; objects must move at orbital velocity or fall.

sp-sec9-u6-L1-Q1.1: This is true because a prograde burn adds kinetic energy, raising the spacecraft to a higher orbit.

sp-sec9-u6-L1-Q2.0: A forward burn adds energy and raises the orbit; it does not lower it.
sp-sec9-u6-L1-Q2.2: The orbit does not instantly become circular; it first becomes elliptical with a higher opposite side.
sp-sec9-u6-L1-Q2.3: A forward burn always changes the orbit by adding energy; orbital mechanics demands a response to any velocity change.

sp-sec9-u6-L1-Q5.0: A retrograde burn removes energy and lowers the orbit, not raises it.

sp-sec9-u6-L1-Q6.0: Burning at perigee raises the opposite side (apogee); it does not lower the perigee itself.
sp-sec9-u6-L1-Q6.2: A prograde burn at perigee makes the orbit more elliptical by raising apogee, not more circular.
sp-sec9-u6-L1-Q6.3: Any velocity change in orbit has an immediate effect on the trajectory; there is no delay to the next orbit.

sp-sec9-u6-L1-Q7.1: Slowing down would lower the orbit, moving the satellite farther from the target altitude.
sp-sec9-u6-L1-Q7.2: Radial burns are inefficient for altitude changes; prograde burns are the standard method.
sp-sec9-u6-L1-Q7.3: Gravity alone will not raise a satellite; an active engine burn is required to change orbits.

sp-sec9-u6-L2-Q1.1: This is true because a Hohmann transfer uses one burn to enter the transfer ellipse and one burn to circularize at the target orbit.

sp-sec9-u6-L2-Q3.0: Hohmann transfers use the minimum fuel of any two-impulse transfer; they are the most fuel-efficient option.
sp-sec9-u6-L2-Q3.2: Hohmann transfers work between any two circular orbits, not just LEO.
sp-sec9-u6-L2-Q3.3: A Hohmann transfer requires exactly 2 burns, not 4.

sp-sec9-u6-L2-Q5.1: This is true because the spacecraft follows the transfer ellipse without any thrust during the coasting phase.

sp-sec9-u6-L2-Q6.0: Flying straight up wastes delta-v fighting gravity and is not how orbital transfers work.
sp-sec9-u6-L2-Q6.2: Spiraling uses low-thrust engines over many orbits, which is slower and uses more total delta-v than a Hohmann transfer.
sp-sec9-u6-L2-Q6.3: Gravity pulls satellites downward; it cannot push them to a higher orbit without an engine burn.
